subtitle: Release Governance · Go/No-Go · CI/CD · AI-Assisted Delivery
hero_title:
The release shipped.
On time. No regressions.
hero_sub: Go/No-Go governance, release calendars, readiness criteria, CAB-style change control, RAID logs, post-release retrospectives. At Crocs I ran exactly what this role describes. I use Claude daily — not as a pilot, as a delivery instrument.

the_case.need: 3+ years release management coordinating software deployments
the_case.answer: 10+ years. Crocs (release governance, end-to-end lifecycle), Eptura (~10 distributed teams, enterprise SaaS PMO), Understood.org (regulated replatform), A&E (~10 concurrent SVOD workstreams), plus OVRSTCH and Stealth AI.
the_case.detail: At Crocs I owned the full release lifecycle — planning through post-launch stabilization — for a global platform rollout at a publicly traded retailer. At Eptura I coordinated release scheduling and dependency management across ~10 distributed teams in an enterprise SaaS PMO. At A&E I held release windows across ~10 concurrent SVOD platform workstreams simultaneously. Ten years of release coordination across regulated, compliance-aware, and high-velocity environments.

the_case.need: AI tools proficiency (Claude named explicitly) for planning, documentation, risk analysis
the_case.answer: Claude Code daily — shipped six production applications this year. Jira AI and Microsoft Copilot embedded in release governance at Crocs: documentation generated, risks analyzed, readiness summaries produced. ~30% overhead reduction with improved output quality.
the_case.detail: When your JD names Claude by name, I am not the typical applicant reading that line. I use Claude to write executable specs, analyze release risks, generate documentation, and summarize readiness status. At Crocs I embedded Claude and Jira AI into weekly release governance workflows — the outputs went directly to leadership. This year I shipped six production web applications using Claude Code as the AI delivery agent, with full CI/CD pipelines via GitHub Actions. The AI tools are not something I plan to use — they are how the work gets done.

the_case.need: Go/No-Go meetings, release calendar, readiness criteria across multiple apps
the_case.answer: Built the Go/No-Go cadence and release calendar at Crocs from scratch. Built Release Radar — the readiness tracking framework that gave leadership real-time cross-environment dependency visibility. Same function at Eptura across ~10 teams.
the_case.detail: The Go/No-Go meeting is not a bureaucratic checkpoint — it is the moment where every dependent team has to confirm their piece of the release is actually ready, or surface the problem before it becomes a production incident. At Crocs I owned that cadence: assembled the dependent teams, ran the readiness checklist, issued the go/no-go decision, and documented it. I built Release Radar specifically to make the incoming signal visible before the meeting, so the Go/No-Go was an informed decision, not a status report.

the_case.need: Cross-team dependency and risk management across multiple pods/applications
the_case.answer: Eptura: dependency management across ~10 distributed teams (India and California). A&E: dependency mapping across Engineering, QA, Content, and Marketing simultaneously across ~10 workstreams. RAID logs at every major engagement.
the_case.detail: Managing cross-team dependencies across five separate Pods is the exact problem I solved at scale at Eptura and A&E. The discipline is: map the dependencies before the sprint starts, surface the conflicts before they stall a release, and own the escalation when a dependency owner is non-responsive. At Crocs I maintained the RAID log and the Release Radar simultaneously — so leadership always knew what was at risk and what the mitigation was.

the_case.need: CI/CD pipeline understanding and modern deployment practices
the_case.answer: Built and owned CI/CD pipelines via GitHub Actions for six production applications this year — build, test, deploy, and live hosting. Coordinated Crocs build→test→UAT→cutover lifecycle. Supported Azure/Kubernetes delivery environments at Eptura and Understood.org.
the_case.detail: I can read a deployment pipeline, understand what a failed build means for a release timeline, and hold a substantive conversation with a DevOps engineer about what's blocking the cutover. At Crocs I coordinated the full build→test→UAT→cutover lifecycle. This year I built and maintained GitHub Actions pipelines for six production applications — architecture, implementation, and deployment all owned end to end.

the_case.need: Influence without authority — drive decisions across teams
the_case.answer: All major roles were client-embedded consulting engagements. The authority was never organizational — it was earned through credibility, preparation, and having the right information at the right time.
the_case.detail: At Crocs I was a contract resource coordinating release decisions across the permanent organization — Engineering, QA, Change Management, and Business all had more organizational standing than I did. The Go/No-Go decisions got made because I built the framework that made the risk visible to everyone simultaneously, and nobody wanted to be the team that held up the release without a documented reason. That is influence without authority: create the information environment where the right decision is also the obvious one.

nodes.label: Claude & Jira AI
nodes.desc: AI-assisted release planning, documentation generation, risk analysis, and readiness summaries
nodes.pm_role: Embedded in release governance at Crocs — ~30% overhead reduction. Claude Code used daily to ship production software. When your JD names Claude, this is the answer.

nodes.label: Jira & Azure DevOps
nodes.desc: Release tracking, dependency management, sprint boards, RAID logs, and executive dashboards
nodes.pm_role: Jira built from scratch at A&E. Standardized across Eptura PMO (~10 teams). Release Radar built in Jira at Crocs. ADO configuration owner at Eptura.

nodes.label: GitHub Actions / CI/CD
nodes.desc: CI/CD pipelines — build, test, deploy — for six production applications; coordinated release lifecycle at Crocs
nodes.pm_role: Hands-on CI/CD via GitHub Actions across six live production apps this year. Coordinated Crocs build→test→UAT→cutover lifecycle. Can engage substantively with DevOps on pipeline blockers.

nodes.label: Confluence & Smartsheet
nodes.desc: Release documentation, readiness criteria, leadership dashboards, and program-level reporting
nodes.pm_role: Release documentation standardized at Eptura PMO. Smartsheet leadership dashboards synthesizing delivery health across ~10 teams. Release calendar and readiness criteria maintained throughout.

nodes.label: Microsoft Copilot
nodes.desc: AI-assisted async stakeholder communication, meeting documentation, and release status reporting
nodes.pm_role: Embedded at Crocs for release reporting and stakeholder communication. Frees release management capacity from document generation to actual coordination and risk management.

when_things_break.scenario: A dependent team signals Not Ready at the Go/No-Go meeting — release is 48 hours out
response: Get the specific blocker in writing before the meeting ends — what exactly is not ready and who owns it
Determine if it's a hard blocker (release cannot proceed) or a mitigable risk (proceed with documented acceptance)
Escalate to leadership immediately with a concrete decision request: delay, de-scope, or accept the risk with owner sign-off
If delay: revise the release calendar, notify all dependent teams with the new timeline, document who made the call
If proceed: document the risk, the mitigation, and the acceptance decision before the deployment window opens

when_things_break.scenario: A cross-Pod dependency is undocumented and surfaces mid-release cycle — two Pods are now blocked on each other
response: Stop assuming the dependency will resolve itself — convene the two Pod leads immediately
Map the exact dependency: what does Pod A need from Pod B, by when, and what happens to the release timeline if it slips
Add it to the RAID log and the release calendar with an explicit owner and resolution date
Build a lightweight synchronization checkpoint between the two Pods for the remainder of this release cycle
Document the gap in the post-release retrospective so the dependency gets captured before the next cycle starts

when_things_break.scenario: AI tool output is used to generate a release risk summary — the output contains an error that reaches a stakeholder
response: Pull the output back immediately — acknowledge the error to the stakeholder directly, don't let it sit
Identify where validation failed: was the AI output reviewed before it went out, or did it bypass the human check?
Correct the summary with verified information and re-send with a clear note on what changed
Add a validation step to the AI workflow — every AI-generated output gets a human review pass before distribution
Apply appropriate judgment and validation to every AI output — that's the standard, not the exception

how_i_run_a_room: Go/No-Go is a decision, not a status meeting — every team confirms ready or surfaces the blocker, on the record
RAID log is live — risks get owners and timelines within 24 hours, not at the next retrospective
Release calendar is visible to all dependent teams, not just the release manager
AI tools compress documentation and risk analysis — human judgment owns every output before it ships
Post-release retrospective findings become the next release's process improvements, not archived notes
Dependency conflicts surface through the release manager before they stall a Pod — not after
Status updates are written, structured, and proactive — not oral reports that require attendance

points: Go/No-Go governance, CAB-style change approval, and SOX-compliant SOD controls
Built Release Radar — release calendar and readiness tracking framework for leadership visibility
Claude and Jira AI embedded in release workflows — ~30% overhead reduction
On-time go-live with clean post-launch stabilization under hard deadline pressure

track_record.role: Senior Delivery Lead — Enterprise SaaS PMO
points: ~10 distributed teams — release scheduling, dependency management, readiness criteria
ADO and Jira dashboards for portfolio-level release health and leadership visibility
35%+ delivery efficiency gain; 20% carryover reduction through release discipline
Post-release retrospectives driving measurable process improvement across the portfolio
~10 concurrent SVOD workstreams (Web, iOS, tvOS, Android, Roku) — simultaneous release coordination
Cross-team dependency management across Engineering, QA, Content, and Marketing
Shipped all platforms against externally committed, publicly visible launch windows
Built Jira release tracking from scratch — zero prior practice across all teams
Six production applications shipped using Claude Code — audio DSP, 3D graphics, game systems, secure Next.js
Full CI/CD via GitHub Actions — build, test, deploy, production hosting for all six
Claude used daily for planning, spec writing, risk analysis, documentation — the workflow this role requires
Proved the AI-assisted delivery model: spec → agent execution → human review → production

before: Go/No-Go meetings surface blockers for the first time — after the release window is already committed
Cross-Pod dependencies are undocumented until they cause a delay
Release documentation takes hours to produce and stakeholders don't read it
Post-release retrospectives happen once — findings go into a doc nobody opens again
after: Go/No-Go decisions are informed, documented, and owned — readiness is visible before the meeting starts
Cross-Pod dependencies are mapped, tracked, and owned with resolution dates before they become blockers
AI tools generate release documentation and risk summaries — human review validates, stakeholders receive
Post-release retrospective findings are the first agenda item for the next release planning cycle

the_ramp.deliverable: Map the five Pods — current release cadence, dependency structure, and existing readiness criteria for each. Meet every engineering lead, QA lead, and DevOps contact. Understand what a release actually looks like today before proposing anything.

Audit the current Go/No-Go process and release calendar. Identify the first three cross-Pod dependency risks that aren't currently tracked. Propose the release tracking framework that gives leadership visibility across all five Pods.
Release calendar published and visible to all Pods. RAID log live. First Go/No-Go meeting run under the new process. AI tools embedded in at least two recurring release documentation workflows.

Consistent release process running across all five Pods. Leadership has real-time visibility into readiness and risk without asking. First post-release retrospective completed with documented process improvements for the next cycle.
